source_display: AI as Brain Crutch for ADHD
evidence: Vrijen shared his personal interest in building AI tools as 'brain crutches' for ADHD—filling gaps between therapy, medication, and coaching
Vrijen warned against the 'death by a thousand cuts' of endless unpaid pilots. Instead, seek paid pilots with scoped changes and identify design partners willing to work with the product as-is
He defined product-market fit using the Supreme Court's obscenity standard: 'you know it when you see it'—when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you obsess over it constantly
A peer-to-peer strategic conversation between Vrijen Attawar and Chris Hanson. Chris is building an ADHD support product called '360'
Chris Hanson, Founder / Small Business Owner at Lifeskillsadvocate, building edtech product (360) for executive function coaching

Chris credited Joe Coleman, described as a 'tech handyman' and jack-of-all-trades, for development support. Vrijen shared his own similar path—non-technical background, first year characterized by face-plants and misplaced trust in technical partners
Vrijen invited Chris to join the Future of Higher Education group (6,000 members globally, ~1,000 in NYC, ~1,000 in Boston), where Vrijen serves as NYC co-lead

source_display: Build in Public for Emotive Products
evidence: Vrijen recommended building in public given the emotive nature of executive function coaching. He suggested leveraging Chris's 65,000-email list for distribution
The core concept is a 'marketplace of all possible futures' - allowing users to 'jump into' career simulations mapped to real jobs and companies
Max used the metaphor of a 'neopet' - anytime you train or deploy your AI requires 'energy,' which is where they monetize through usage-based pricing
Maximilian Kwok is Founder at Dayoneme, building a marketplace of 'possible futures' - career-focused simulations

Careerspan pivoting to generate revenue through India recruiting partnership before targeting mid-year acquisition. The consensus is to aim for a sale mid-year but spend the next few months generating revenue first

Maximilian observed that engineers more than one step removed from the product were most vulnerable. Visible links between individual work and the product/service being sold are increasingly essential in 2026

source_display: Assessments Must Ground in Business Value
evidence: He criticized tools like HackerRank for not grounding results in actual business value - passing an algorithm assessment doesn't prove someone can deliver value to the company
Maximilian argued these roles are 'done' in the current AI-enabled landscape, with many PM positions facing similar elimination unless they manage P&L and have product experience

source_display: SAT Mentality Flattens Humans to Two Axes
evidence: Vrijen used the example of parents obsessing over SATs because numbers are concrete and discrete, but noted this flattens humans to two axes inappropriately
He introduced context engineering as the foundational practice: not prompting the AI directly, but cultivating the context it uses to generate desired outputs. He outlined three strategic levels
Vrijen argued the core issue isn't a technology or tactics problem but a mental model problem—users collect techniques without understanding how they integrate into effective systems
Threshold rubrics: Ask the AI to create an evaluation rubric, fill it out, assign scores, and iterate output until achieving the highest level
Offensive and defensive prompting: Expansive generation (expand option set) vs. critical tightening (attack, break down, shore up)
Ben embraced the 'unhobbling' concept from the Anthropic/Claude team—recognizing the AI is capable of more than you initially realize
Ben Guo (co-founder of Zo Computer and Vrijen as a power Zo user). Zo Computer - managed AI personal assistant platform

source_display: Job Security is Dead, Build Career Security
evidence: Logan's conviction: 'job security is dead and you need to be building career security for yourself... it has a lot to do with being findable and being visible'
Logan demonstrated deep analysis of 18 years of LinkedIn data using Claude Code, having exported 48 CSV files containing 5,000+ messages, connections, search history
Social capital proxy: Inbound versus outbound connection ratio as a measure of professional standing
Posting correlation: Documented 1,800% increase in inbound connections when increasing posting frequency, strong evidence for visibility strategies
Logan's metacognitive patterns: never accept the first answer, consistently ask 'what else?' and 'delight me'
He positioned this as the future of coding: planning systems through narrative structure rather than pure code alone, with Zo planning enhanced UI for long-range task planning
Ben highlighted text/email workflows enabling parallel work during passive moments (airport lines, cab rides), using Claude Code via text message to fix code branches
An AI productivity workshop presented by Careerspan (Vrijen Attawar and Logan Currie) and Zo Computer (Ben Guo)
An AI productivity workshop presented by Careerspan (Vrijen Attawar and Logan Currie) and Zo Computer (Ben Guo)
